tema: Adverbs of Manner — quickly, carefully & comparatives

banco_oraciones.oracion: Ben sings loudly in the shower.
Sofía works faster than anyone in her office.
He doesn't drive carefully at night.
They didn't work hard on this project.
I can't wake up early on Mondays.
Who works the most carefully in your class?
Do you sing well? No, I don't.
Does Ben dance badly? Yes, he does... enthusiastically.
Did they arrive early? Yes, they did.

interaccion: Sofía: I work **quickly**, but I check everything **carefully**. Two adverbs, one designer.
Ben: I sing **loudly**. The neighbors say I sing **badly**. Both things can be true.
Kenji: And you can say it two ways: 'I **quickly** learned salsa' or 'I learned salsa **quickly**'. Before the verb, or at the end!
Abuela Carmen: And I dance **better than** all of you. **The most gracefully**, in fact. It was proven in 1975.
inicio.definicion_pragmatica: Adverbs of manner say HOW something is done — most end in -ly, and they can go before the verb or at the end of the sentence.

filas: good → well · fast → fast · hard → hard
She speaks well · He runs fast
faster than · more carefully than · better / worse
She works more carefully than me
He works hard ≠ He hardly works

ejercicios.afirmacion: Adverbs can go before the verb or at the end of the sentence: 'She quickly finished' / 'She finished quickly'.

ejercicios.respuesta: Ben sings loudly in the shower

ejercicios.instruccion: Move the adverb before the verb.

ejercicios.pregunta: Do you speak English well? (short answer)
Did you wake up early today? (short answer, No)

guion: Sofía: Team meeting! Kenji, you learned Spanish quickly. What's the secret?
Kenji: I practice daily, I listen carefully, and I make mistakes happily.
Ben: Then I'm doing great — I make mistakes more frequently than anyone!
Abuela Carmen: Making mistakes is fine, Ben. But you cut the onions dangerously. Slowly, my dear. Slowly.
Sofía: Meeting conclusion: practice daily, listen carefully, and cut onions slowly.

comprension.pregunta: What is the advice for Ben about cutting onions?

reading.texto: How do you learn a language well? Kenji wrote his method on one page. 'Listen carefully — more carefully than you speak. Practice daily, even badly: five bad minutes beat zero perfect ones. Speak bravely; people correct you kindly more often than you expect. Write slowly at first, then faster every week. And most importantly: compare yourself patiently — you will not improve as quickly as you want, but you will improve more steadily than you think.' Abuela Carmen read it and nodded wisely: 'The boy writes well.'

preguntas.afirmacion: Kenji says you should listen more carefully than you speak.
He recommends practicing only when you can do it perfectly.
He says people usually correct you kindly.
Abuela Carmen thinks Kenji writes badly.
